text.title: python-basics Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for python-basics. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use built-in generic syntax for type hints: list[int], dict[str, int], set[str], tuple[int, ...] — never from typing import List, Dict, Set, Tuple.
rule.rationale: Built-in generics (PEP 585, Python 3.9+) are the current standard. The typing module aliases were deprecated in 3.9 and should trigger ruff UP006/UP035 warnings. Removing them reduces import noise.

rule.statement: Use X | Y union syntax instead of Union[X, Y] and X | None instead of Optional[X] — requires Python 3.10+ or from __future__ import annotations.
rule.rationale: PEP 604 pipe union reads as "or" and is shorter. Optional[X] obscures intent; X | None is explicit about nullability. ruff UP007 auto-upgrades on --fix.

rule.statement: Use tuple for fixed-arity heterogeneous sequences and dict keys; use list for variable-length homogeneous sequences.
rule.rationale: Tuple immutability makes it hashable (usable as dict key) and communicates fixed structure. Lists communicate mutability and variable length. Mixing the two semantics confuses readers.

rule.statement: Use set for membership tests and deduplication; use frozenset when the set must be hashable or passed to functions as a contract.
rule.rationale: set lookup is O(1) vs O(n) for list. frozenset is the immutable counterpart, suitable as dict key or function argument when mutation must be prevented.

rule.statement: Prefer collections.defaultdict over dict.setdefault() for grouped accumulation; prefer collections.Counter over manual frequency counting.
rule.rationale: defaultdict eliminates the "check-then-insert" pattern in loop bodies. Counter is purpose-built for frequency tallies and exposes .most_common() without sorting manually.

rule.statement: Use list/dict/set comprehensions for single-expression transforms; use a for-loop when the body contains side effects, early returns, or complex logic.
rule.rationale: Comprehensions signal "transform a collection" — the intent is clear at a glance. Comprehensions with if/else branches, nested calls, or appended side effects become harder to read than a for-loop with a comment.

rule.statement: Use generator expressions ((x for x in ...)) instead of list comprehensions when the result is consumed once (passed to sum(), any(), max(), etc.).
rule.rationale: Generator expressions are lazy — they avoid materializing an intermediate list in memory. sum(x*x for x in data) is both shorter and more memory-efficient than sum([x*x for x in data]).

rule.statement: Use match/case (Python 3.10+) for dispatch on structure, not just value equality. Use case _: exhaustive fallthrough instead of a final bare else.
rule.rationale: Pattern matching deconstructs data (sequences, mappings, classes) directly in the case clause. case _: is the structural equivalent of else: but scales to multiple pattern arms without nested if/elif chains.

rule.statement: Annotate all public function signatures with type hints including the return type. Use -> None for procedures that have no return value.
rule.rationale: Without return-type annotations, mypy skips the function body in its inferred-return analysis. Explicit annotations make the contract clear and improve LLM code generation accuracy.

rule.statement: Never use mutable objects as default argument values. Use None as the default and initialize inside the body.
rule.rationale: Default argument values are evaluated once at function definition time, not per call. A mutable default like def f(items=[]) shares the same list across all calls — a classic Python footgun.

rule.statement: Use keyword-only arguments (def f(*, name: str)) for clarity when a function has more than two positional parameters or when argument names carry intent.
rule.rationale: Keyword-only arguments prevent positional ordering mistakes at call sites. They also make function signatures self-documenting and resistant to refactoring breaks when argument order changes.

rule.statement: Always apply @functools.wraps(func) in decorator wrappers to preserve the wrapped function's __name__, __doc__, and signature.
rule.rationale: Without functools.wraps, introspection tools (help(), Sphinx, FastAPI's OpenAPI) see the wrapper's metadata instead of the original function's. This breaks auto-generated docs and tracebacks.

rule.statement: Use @dataclass for data containers with typed fields instead of bare __init__ methods or named tuples with many fields.
rule.rationale: Dataclasses auto-generate __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ from field declarations. @dataclass(frozen=True) produces an immutable, hashable value object without manual __hash__.

rule.statement: Use field(default_factory=list) for mutable defaults in dataclasses — never assign [] or {} directly as a field default.
rule.rationale: Dataclass field defaults are shared the same way function argument defaults are. The default_factory creates a fresh instance per object. A bare [] default raises ValueError at class definition time in modern Python.

rule.statement: Use Protocol for structural duck-typing contracts between modules instead of ABC inheritance. Add @runtime_checkable only when isinstance() checks at runtime are actually needed.
rule.rationale: Protocol satisfies the Open/Closed principle: a class satisfies a Protocol without importing it. ABC requires explicit inheritance, creating import coupling. @runtime_checkable adds per-call overhead — omit it for hot paths.

rule.statement: Prefer EAFP (Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission) over LBYL (Look Before You Leap) for operations where the failure path is genuinely exceptional.
rule.rationale: EAFP eliminates race conditions in the LBYL check-then-act window. In a concurrent system, a "key in dict" check before "dict[key]" can fail between the two steps. EAFP also avoids double-lookup in hash tables.

rule.statement: Keep try blocks as narrow as possible — wrap only the exact operation that can raise the expected exception, not the surrounding logic.
rule.rationale: A wide try block silently catches unexpected exceptions from unrelated code. If process(data) raises ValueError for a different reason than int(data["value"]), the except clause hides the bug.

rule.statement: Never use bare except: or except Exception: without re-raising or logging the exception with its full traceback.
rule.rationale: Bare except swallows SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt, and GeneratorExit. Bare except Exception swallows programming errors that should crash loudly. Silent swallowing produces impossible-to-debug state.

rule.statement: Define a base exception class per module or app (e.g., class OrderError(Exception): ...), then subclass for each specific error. Never raise bare Exception from library or service code.
rule.rationale: A base exception per domain allows callers to catch all domain errors with one clause while still distinguishing specific cases. Bare Exception cannot be caught selectively without catching everything.

rule.statement: Use dict.get(key, default) when a missing key is a normal expected case with a simple default. Use try/except KeyError when missing means an error condition that needs explicit handling.
rule.rationale: dict.get() is idiomatic for optional lookup with defaults. try/except KeyError signals that absence is unexpected and requires different behavior — not just a fallback value. Mixing them leads to inconsistent error semantics.

rule.statement: Use uv as the single tool for Python version management, virtual environments, and package installation. Do not use pip, venv, or pyenv alongside uv in new projects.
rule.rationale: uv is 10-100x faster than pip (warm cache), manages Python versions itself (uv python install), and uses a global cache to avoid redundant downloads. One tool eliminates the pip/pip-tools/pyenv/virtualenv coordination complexity.

rule.statement: Pin the Python version per project with a .python-version file (uv python pin 3.12). Keep it in sync with requires-python in pyproject.toml.
rule.rationale: Without a pinned version, uv sync picks the highest available Python. Team members on different minor versions encounter subtle behavior differences (stdlib additions, syntax changes). .python-version + requires-python provide two layers of enforcement.

rule.statement: Consolidate all project config in pyproject.toml: project metadata, build system (hatchling), ruff, mypy, and pytest settings. Do not use setup.cfg, setup.py, .flake8, or mypy.ini.
rule.rationale: Multiple config files fragment project setup. pyproject.toml is the PEP 517/518 standard and all major tools (ruff, mypy, pytest, hatchling, uv) support it. Consolidation reduces cognitive overhead when onboarding.

rule.statement: Use ruff for both linting and formatting (ruff check + ruff format). Enable rule groups: E (errors), F (pyflakes), I (isort), B (bugbear), UP (pyupgrade), C4 (comprehensions), T20 (no-print).
rule.rationale: Ruff replaces Flake8 + Black + isort + pyupgrade in a single binary that runs in milliseconds. Rule group UP auto-upgrades deprecated typing syntax to modern equivalents. T20 blocks accidental print() in production code.

rule.statement: Run ruff check . --fix before committing to auto-fix all safe-fixable violations. Set ruff format . in pre-commit hooks to enforce consistent formatting.
rule.rationale: Auto-fix eliminates manual correction of mechanical style issues. Pre-commit hooks make formatting non-negotiable — no "I forgot" in code review. CI runs ruff check without --fix to catch unfixable violations.

rule.statement: Run mypy with strict = true in CI. For new projects, start strict from day one. For legacy codebases, enable strict incrementally per module using [[tool.mypy.overrides]].
rule.rationale: Strict mode enables disallow_untyped_defs, warn_return_any, and no_implicit_optional — the most impactful flags. Adding strict to an existing codebase all at once generates hundreds of errors; per-module overrides let you clean up incrementally.

rule.statement: Exclude migrations/ and tests/ from strict mode via mypy overrides. Tests use dynamic fixtures; migrations are generated code — both produce false positives under strict.
rule.rationale: Django migrations use dynamic field references that mypy cannot resolve without django-stubs. Test files use pytest.fixture and conftest patterns that produce untyped-def noise. Excluding them focuses strict mode on production code.
